conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. 

To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you some questions. Let me start by understanding your situation:

**Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'm inquiring about my child. I have one child - their name is Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you! I'd like to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me start by asking:

**How old is Alex?**

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since Alex is 21 years old, I need to understand more about their circumstances, as Child Benefit eligibility typically extends beyond age 16 only in certain situations.

**Is Alex currently in full-time approved education or training?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in education. They work full-time actually.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. Let me gather the remaining details to make a full assessment.

**Do you live with Alex, and are you responsible for them?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for sharing that information. Before I provide my final assessment, let me just read the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Based on the official guidance I've reviewed, I now have enough information to make a determination about your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on my assessment of your situation, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why:

Child Benefit normally applies to children under 16. For young people aged 16 or over, Child Benefit can continue **only if** they are:
- Under 20 years old, **and**
- In approved education or training

Since Alex is 21 years old, they are over the age limit of 20 for extended Child Benefit eligibility. Additionally, Alex is working full-time rather than being in approved education or training. According to the rules, Child Benefit stops when a child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training.

I'm sorry this isn't the outcome you were hoping for. If you have any other questions or if Alex's circumstances change in the future, please feel free to ask.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 21 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 21, which is 20 or over
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child receives qualifying benefits (e.g. UC, ESA) in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 21, which is 20 or over; Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education; Child receives qualifying benefits (e.g. UC, ESA) in their own right
circumstances: Child is 21, which is 20 or over
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child receives qualifying benefits (e.g. UC, ESA) in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 21 years old and is working full-time. According to the eligibility rules, Child Benefit normally applies to children under 16. For children aged 16 or over, eligibility can continue only if the child is under 20 AND stays in approved education or training. Since Alex is 21 years old, they are over the age limit of 20 for extended eligibility. Additionally, Alex is not in approved education or training - they are working full-time. The rules also state that Child Benefit stops if your child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. Therefore, Alex does not meet the age or education/training criteria for Child Benefit eligibility.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 21 years old and working full-time. Child Benefit eligibility ends at age 16 unless the child remains in approved education or training, in which case it can continue until age 20. Since Alex is over 20 and not in education or training, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit.

For children aged 16 or over, eligibility can continue only if the child is under 20 AND stays in approved education or training. Since Alex is 21 years old, they are over the age limit of 20 for extended eligibility. Additionally, Alex is not in approved education or training - they are working full-time. The rules also state that Child Benefit stops if your child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. Therefore, Alex does not meet the age or education/training criteria for Child Benefit eligibility.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 21 years old and working full-time. Child Benefit eligibility ends at age 16 unless the child remains in approved education or training, in which case it can continue until age 20. Since Alex is over 20 and not in education or training, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit.
